note: Navigation and validation manifest only — never a normative restatement of any check. Normative text: governance/mandate/part1.md. | v2.0: Track C (C-01..C-40) activated by DR-004.

checks.title: The verification gate on every production change
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: build the gate; remove every bypass; give the gate its own test suite. Do NOT substitute a human approver — that option is not available here, and at this volume it would degrade into a rubber stamp within a week (latency of a review, none of the assurance). Then re-verify retroactively every change that reached production ungated, largest blast radius first, by re-running the completed gate against it. Note: A-39 escalates to STOP-SHIP if A-01 also fails.

checks.title: A test suite that can actually fail
checks.conditional_escalations: Escalates to STOP-SHIP if mutation testing shows the suite cannot detect injected faults. On failure: delete or repair assertion-free and self-mocking tests (a test that cannot fail manufactures false assurance); add the missing layer; quarantine flakes with an owning role and a deadline; wire mutation testing as a gate. The mutation score is the true measure of the system's safety margin — publish it next to uptime.

checks.title: Deterministic and probabilistic assertions kept apart
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: convert judged checks into verifiable ones, aggressively. Every check made deterministic is one less place where the system grades its own homework — the only structural defence, absent a reviewer, against a closed loop of self-assessment.

checks.title: A specification exists, is testable, has non-goals, and gates merges
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: reverse-engineer the specification from the running system, reconcile it against the stated product intent (the in-command function — the one thing legitimately requiring a person, because it is authorship, not review), freeze it, then write the missing tests. In a system with no reviewer, an unspecified change is an unauthorised change. Ambiguity is the input that produces confident, wrong output — the specification is the leverage point, not the prompt.

checks.title: Domain boundaries and a consistent vocabulary
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: produce the context map from the code as built, unify the vocabulary with a mechanical rename, record the owning role, and add the fitness function — without it the boundaries erode silently and nobody is watching.

checks.title: Version control, batch size and a rollback that has been executed
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write and execute the rollback runbook, then wire it to a signal. An untested rollback is a hypothesis; a rollback only a human can trigger is a rollback that will be triggered late.

checks.title: Clone, churn and refactoring signature
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: add the tripwire; de-duplicate the largest clone clusters first, because they are also where the clonal defects live (Section 0, hazard 3).

checks.title: Security scanning is the control, not a first pass before one
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: wire the scanners in as blocking, triage, fix criticals, and rotate anything the secret scan surfaces (see B-06). Treat every line of machine-authored code as untrusted until an executable check has proven the specific property you care about — 'untrusted until reviewed' reduces here to simply 'untrusted', so the scanners are not a first pass before the real check; they ARE the real check.

checks.title: Architecture as decided, not as accreted
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write the decision records from the system as built — one page each: context, decision, consequences, alternatives rejected. Flag every coupling that was accidental rather than chosen, and encode the ones that matter as tests.

checks.title: Injection-resistant architecture, not injection-resistant wording
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: do not write a filter and call it fixed, and do not propose that a human confirm the tool call — the same false remediation wearing a different hat. Remove the architectural capability instead. See C-07 and C-08 — this is the design-time half; B-20 and B-22 are the runtime and enforcement halves.

checks.title: Least-privilege topology for every agent and tool (design)
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: build the scope matrix, set it deny-by-default, and delete every capability nobody can justify and every irreversible capability nobody can validate. B-22 enforces this at deploy time.

checks.title: Technical debt and the comprehension problem
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: produce the reconstruction map. For every module that traces to no intent: either write the specification it turns out to satisfy and pin it with tests, or delete it — there is no honest third option. Code that exists for no recorded reason is this system's version of a rumour: plausible, unattributable, impossible to correct.

checks.title: Enforced coding standards
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: add the tooling, then fix violations one rule per change set so each is independently verifiable. Then add the suppression counter — without it, the standards will be disabled one file at a time while the build stays green throughout.

checks.title: A written policy for how AI builds and maintains this
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write the policy; define the tiers; instrument the workflow so the next claim about productivity is measured rather than felt.

checks.title: The boundary between prototype and production
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: close every ungated path, then re-gate retroactively whatever came through them, largest blast radius first. Escalate with A-01 and A-39.

checks.title: The last mile: stubs, edge cases and integration
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: list every stub, rank by exposure, close or gate them behind a feature flag that is off — then add the lint rule so the class cannot recur.

checks.title: Non-functional requirements, specified and measured
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write the table. An NFR with no number is a wish; an NFR with no test is a wish with a number.

checks.title: Model and agent architecture chosen deliberately
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write the record; collapse multi-agent structures where a single call demonstrably suffices. Prefer the simplest architecture that works.

checks.title: API contracts that match the implementation
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: regenerate the spec from the code, then close the gaps IN THE CODE, not in the spec — and wire the drift check so the gap cannot silently reopen.

checks.title: Prompts and retrieval configuration are code, and pass the same gate
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: move them into version control and put them behind the same gate as code. These artifacts define behaviour as surely as code does. A prompt that can change in production without passing the gate is a production code change with no gate — precisely the thing this entire document exists to prevent.

checks.title: Context, retrieval and memory architecture
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: trim the context, add the measurement, schedule the re-index, and alert on staleness rather than trusting that someone will remember.

checks.title: User outcomes, performance and accessibility
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: fix accessibility blockers first (legally exposed and cheap), wire the automated gate, then instrument outcomes.

checks.title: Data architecture and ownership
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: document the schemas from the data as it exists, add migrations, wire the drift and quality checks, and record the owning roles.

checks.title: The system operates and recovers itself
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: wire the automated recovery. 'A human will notice' is not a recovery strategy — it is the absence of one. The old failure mode was build-it-and-nobody-runs-it; the new one is build-it-and-nothing-recovers-it, and it is worse, because it looks fine right up until it does not.

checks.title: Input validation at every boundary
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: add validation at the boundary; eliminate every string-concatenated query; sweep for clones of the pattern — a validation gap is never a single route (Section 0, hazard 3).

checks.title: Error handling that does not lie
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: remove the masking first, then add the lint rule so it cannot come back. A system that hides its failures cannot be operated; every metric collected from it is a lie, and in a system with no reader those lies are never contradicted.

checks.title: Non-functional requirements that are specific to AI
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: set the budgets, then enforce them where the model cannot reach. An unbudgeted AI feature is an uncapped liability (see B-08); a budget that is only monitored will be exceeded by an agent at 3am with nobody watching.

checks.title: Dependency topology and blast radius
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: add the resilience primitives to the critical path first, then prove them by injecting the failure (B-29). A fallback that has never been exercised is a comment.

checks.title: Build versus buy versus open source — including the model
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write the records; build the abstraction seam; test the fallback. Models are retired on the vendor's timetable, not yours (see B-36) — and the deprecation notice will arrive in an inbox nobody is watching.

checks.title: Non-functional trade-offs analysed, not stumbled into
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: write them down, then check the code actually implements the position that was chosen — and where it can be asserted, assert it as a test.

checks.title: Unit economics decided at design time
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: build the model now; it will tell you which architectural choices you cannot afford to keep.

checks.title: Documentation that is true — and executable
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: rewrite documentation to match reality, wire the execution check, then delete anything you cannot verify. False documentation is worse than none: it is the thing the break-glass responder will trust at the worst possible moment — and they will be the only human in the room.

checks.title: Maintainability without a maintainer
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: the remedy for bus factor zero is not a human — it is artifacts: an executable specification, tests that survive mutation, decision records, and an agent-instruction file good enough that a cold agent succeeds without guessing. Build them, then run the cold-start test again. The success rate is the number that matters — the only honest answer to 'is this system maintainable' in a world where nobody maintains it by hand.

checks.title: Autonomy levels, deterministic gates and a kill switch that fires itself
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: define the levels, remove the capabilities you cannot validate, build the automatic halt, then trip it. The failure mode you are guarding against is no longer rubber-stamping — it is a system with no stamp at all and nothing to stop it.

checks.title: Runtime containment without an operator
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: do NOT add a human to the queue — remove the queue. Either the action is safe enough to take automatically (validate it deterministically and take it) or it is not (remove the capability). A queue with nobody in it is the worst of both worlds: the latency of a gate and the assurance of none.

checks.title: Calibration of the verification pipeline
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: publish the catch rate. It is the most honest number in this entire audit, and in a system with no reviewer it is the only number that tells you what your green build is worth.

checks.title: Takeover readiness
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: produce the takeover pack and normalise the patterns. This check is what stands between the organisation and being permanently dependent on the tool that built the system — and with no human reviewer, that dependency is already deeper than anyone has admitted.

checks.title: Provenance and licensing of the code that shipped
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: scan, replace or clear the offending code, and get the IP position written down. In an acquisition this is a valuation and indemnity issue, not a technicality.

checks.title: The verification loop must not be self-referential
checks.conditional_escalations: Escalates to STOP-SHIP if A-01 also fails. On failure: break the self-reference, not the automation — a closed machine loop is not the problem; an UNWITNESSED one is. Introduce a different model, a different vendor, a falsifying objective, and above all a deterministic arbiter that does not care what any model thinks. Check the test-generation path first: if the same model wrote both the code and its test, you do not have a test — you have a mirror.

checks.title: Energy and carbon as a design constraint
checks.conditional_escalations: On failure: measure, or document immateriality. Do not quote a number you cannot reproduce.

checks.title: The pipeline actually gates
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Epigraph: with no reviewer behind it, the pipeline is not a safety net — it is the entire safety system. Targets: deploy on demand; lead time to production <1 day; change-failure rate 0–15%; failed-deployment recovery <1 hour; the pipeline blocks on a failing test OR a failing security gate; every soft-fail removed, and the gate's blocking behaviour proven by test rather than asserted by configuration. AI raises change volume and simultaneously raises instability — without gates that bite, that volume goes straight into production, and here it goes there unread. On failure: make the gates blocking; remove every soft-fail; then re-run the seeded-defect calibration (Phase 2) to confirm the pipeline now actually catches them — a pipeline you have not calibrated is a pipeline you are trusting on faith.

checks.title: A paved road an agent can walk
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: a documented paved road walked end-to-end by an agent that did not write it; self-service provisioning with no human in the request path; one-command bootstrap from a clean clone; the platform treated as a product with tracked adoption. A high-quality internal platform is the single biggest amplifier of whether AI helps or hurts an organisation — it is the layer where standards actually get distributed, and in a system with no reviewer it is the only layer where they can be. On failure: fix the bootstrap first; everything else in this track depends on being able to reproduce the environment reliably, and every agent that cannot will improvise.

checks.title: Observability that reaches root cause
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: three pillars via OpenTelemetry; golden-signal dashboards; mean-time-to-detect and mean-time-to-restore tracked as real numbers; telemetry structured and queryable by machine, not merely renderable to a human eye. A machine-accelerated system that you cannot debug is a system you cannot own — and one nobody reads is a system you cannot debug by intuition. On failure: instrument — start with the critical user journey, not with everything.

checks.title: Dependencies that exist, are pinned, and were vetted
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1, S3 — the highest-yield check in the document. Targets: software-composition analysis on every build; lockfile/hash pinning; a pre-install registry-existence check as a blocking gate; license scanning; transitive-risk gating. Measured scale of the risk: across 576,000 code samples from 16 models, 19.7% of package references were hallucinated — 205,474 unique non-existent packages, open-source models ~21.7% and commercial ~5.2%, with roughly 43% of hallucinations recurring across reruns, making them a stable, profilable target. Attackers pre-register the names models reliably invent; the existence check is non-negotiable because an invented package name is exactly what a human reviewer would have caught in ten seconds and nothing else in this system ever will. On failure: pin everything by hash; add the existence check and the allowlist to CI as blocking steps; treat any dependency you cannot account for as compromised until proven otherwise.

checks.title: Models, prompts and agents have a lifecycle
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S9. Targets: a model and prompt registry; a continuous evaluation pipeline; a deprecation runbook that executes; the whole thing mapped to your secure-development and AI-management control set. The registry is also the provenance spine (S9): without it, C-37's accountability chain cannot be reconstructed for any AI behaviour. On failure: build the registry — untracked prompt and model changes are the single most common source of 'it used to work' in these systems, and with nobody reading diffs, 'it used to work' is the only symptom you will get.

checks.title: Secrets and machine identity
checks.conditional_escalations: STOP-SHIP. Tags: S6, S9. Targets: vaulting with automated rotation; short-lived workload identity (OAuth 2.1, token exchange per RFC 8693); no secrets in repositories, ever; scoped credentials per agent, per environment. Scale: 28.65 million new hard-coded secrets appeared in public commits in a single year (34% YoY rise), and machine identities outnumber human ones by roughly two orders of magnitude — in this system essentially every identity is a machine identity, so this is not a niche concern, it is the identity model. On failure: ROTATE FIRST — revoke and reissue every exposed credential before touching the repository; only then purge history and move to a vault. Removing the secret from code without rotating it is not a fix — it is the appearance of one.

checks.title: Every model and agent run is traceable and replayable
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S9. Targets: spans following the OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions (operation name, request model, token usage, tool execution, agent invocation); a trace identifier linking agent → tool → response; retention long enough to investigate an incident discovered weeks later. This trace is also an accountability artifact (S9, C-37): it is what the organisation produces when asked what the system did and why. On failure: instrument the spans before attempting any AI-quality work — you cannot fix what you cannot replay, and you cannot answer for what you cannot reconstruct.

checks.title: No path may consume unbounded tokens or money
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S6. Targets: per-user and per-tenant token and rate quotas; a maximum response length; a recursion and tool-call-depth cap; cost-per-request as a monitored signal with spike alerting AND an automatic cut-off that fires without a human decision (S6) — a cap that pages someone is not a cap. On failure: add the caps before anything else in this section; unbounded consumption is a denial-of-wallet vulnerability, trivially reachable through a single successful injection.

checks.title: Signed provenance for everything shipped, including models
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S9. Targets: hardened, non-falsifiable build provenance (SLSA Build L3) for externally distributed artifacts, L2 minimum internally; an SBOM in CI (CycloneDX or SPDX); an AI-BOM covering models, datasets, adapters and weights; signed attestations (in-toto / Sigstore-style). These are becoming procurement and legal requirements, not best practices — and in this operating model they carry a second job: the attestation is the accountability record that a human signature used to be (S9, C-37). On failure: generate, sign, and — critically — verify at deploy with a policy that fails closed. An archived attestation nobody checks is paperwork, and in this system nobody checks anything by hand.

checks.title: Evaluation gates in the pipeline
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1, S2. Targets: a golden-dataset regression suite built from real traffic and real failures; threshold gates that block on regression; the judge model different from the model under test, and from a different vendor (S2); documented mitigations for judge bias — position, verbosity, self-preference — see C-14. Strong model judges reach over 80% agreement with human raters (roughly human-human agreement), so judging is a viable gate — but only once you have measured that agreement against an externally-sourced label set, and only for the properties you could not make deterministic (A-03). On failure: build the golden set from real traffic and real failures, not from generated examples — a golden set the system wrote for itself is not an evaluation, it is a self-portrait.

checks.title: Rollback for code, prompts and models
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S5, S8. Targets: failed-deployment recovery under 1 hour; versioned and pinned model and prompt artifacts with one-command rollback; the rollback wired to the automatic abort criteria (S8, B-18). Specific hazard: a silent provider-side model update can present exactly like a code regression — without a pinned version to roll back to, you will spend the incident debugging your own innocent code, and in this system 'you' is an agent with no memory of yesterday. On failure: pin the models (B-13), version the prompts, script the rollback, rehearse it, then wire it to the signal.

checks.title: Runtime defence
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: a web application firewall or equivalent; runtime threat detection; documented patch SLAs by severity AND evidence they are met by an automated process; scheduled secret rotation. Remember: an AI application is still a web application underneath — the conventional attack surface has not gone anywhere just because the interesting risks moved. On failure: patch, then deploy the missing controls, then automate the patching so the next window does not depend on attention.

checks.title: Pinned model versions — no floating aliases
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: pinned provider model snapshots — never a 'latest' alias; a versioned prompt registry; immutable deploys with rollback; a CI check that fails the build on any unpinned model reference, so the class cannot recur. Providers migrate retired model deployments to successors on their own schedule: a floating alias means your application's behaviour can change while your code does not — the one failure mode no amount of code review would ever have caught, and that nothing in a machine-maintained system will catch either unless you pin. On failure: pin every model reference today — a one-line change that prevents an entire class of unexplainable incident.

checks.title: Governance over prompt and configuration changes
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: configuration-as-code behind the identical gate as code; an audit trail on every prompt change (which identity, when, what, against which specification); where hot-swap is genuinely needed, deploy is decoupled from release BUT the change still passes the gate and is still recorded. On failure: an ungoverned hot-swap path is not a review bypass — it is a GATE bypass, which in this system is the same as no control at all. Close it or route it through the gate; there is no third option, because there is no reviewer to catch what comes through it.

checks.title: Guardrails are deployed artifacts, and they fail closed
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: versioned guardrail artifacts with their own tests; fail-closed by default, verified by killing the service; an online-evaluation feedback loop; input and output filtering with measured false-positive and false-negative rates on YOUR OWN traffic (see C-15). Cautionary case: a real zero-click exfiltration in a major vendor's product defeated a purpose-built injection classifier, link redaction AND content-security policy — three layers, each failing open in its own way, in a product with far more human oversight than this one has. On failure: make it fail closed, then measure its actual detection rates on your own traffic. Never accept a vendor's headline detection number as evidence.

checks.title: Cost attribution and financial operations
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S6. Targets: cost attribution and chargeback by tenant/feature; normalised billing data; unit cost (cost per request) tracked as a first-class metric alongside latency and error rate, with a hard ceiling enforced in infrastructure (S6, B-08). On failure: tag resources, then build cost-per-request, then cap it. Nearly every organisation running AI in production now manages AI spend explicitly; running it blind is the outlier position, and running it blind AND autonomously is a novel one.

checks.title: Infrastructure as code, reconciled from version control
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: infrastructure-as-code covering 100% of infrastructure; immutable images; reconcile-from-git; automated drift detection that alerts AND reverts, not one that merely reports. On failure: import the hand-made resources into code. Anything not in code will be lost in the next incident, and it will not come back the same — and nobody remembers how it was made, because nobody made it.

checks.title: Progressive delivery with an automatic abort
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S8 — this mechanism is doctrine in this operating model, not an optimisation. Targets: canary or blue/green with automated rollback triggers; ring or percentage rollout; explicit abort criteria on error rate, latency, evaluation score, cost and guardrail trip rate; the abort tested by firing it, with a recorded time-to-abort. On failure: add the canary and wire the abort to a real signal, not to a human noticing. Nobody is going to notice.

checks.title: Service objectives with an error budget that bites
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: a 99.9% objective implies roughly 43 minutes of budget per month — know the number; multi-window burn-rate alerts (e.g. 14.4x burn over one hour); a written error-budget policy that freezes releases automatically when the budget is spent — enforced in the pipeline, as a gate, not as a decision (S1). On failure: define the objectives against what users actually need, then wire the freeze into the pipeline so it constrains the system without asking anyone's permission.

checks.title: Runtime detection AND containment of injection and exfiltration
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S5, S6 — the highest-severity AI-specific runtime control in the document. Targets: monitoring of all model-influenced outbound URLs, markdown links and image beacons; taint-tracking with a policy gate on every exfiltration-capable action; enforcement of the rule that no session may hold more than two of {untrusted input, private data, external communication}; automatic containment on trip — kill the session, revoke the token, quarantine the trace — not a notification; coverage mapped to the injection and disclosure categories of the LLM risk taxonomy and a recognised adversarial-technique knowledge base. Reference incident: a zero-click, real-world production exfiltration (CVSS 9.3) requiring no user action. On failure: block model-controlled outbound rendering; put an egress allowlist in front of anything the model can reach (S6); wire containment to the detection. Detection without automatic containment is worth nothing here, because there is nobody to act on it.

checks.title: The application survives a provider outage
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S5. Targets: an AI-gateway abstraction; automatic provider failover; token-level rate limiting; a documented and TESTED degradation path — including what the user is told while it is degraded. On failure: build the seam. Single-provider coupling is a continuity risk, and forced deprecations make it a SCHEDULED one (see B-36).

checks.title: Agents deployed with least privilege, enforced
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S6 — the control that bounds the damage of every failure elsewhere in this document. Targets: a scoped identity per agent (never a shared service account, never a borrowed human session); sandboxed or containerised execution; a connector/tool-server allowlist with signed manifests and version pinning; tools deny-by-default; the allowlist stored where the agent has no write access (see B-35 — an agent that can edit its own allowlist has no allowlist). Excessive agency and tool misuse map directly to real 2025–2026 incidents in which agents were induced to exfiltrate private repositories through entirely legitimate tool calls. On failure: revoke every scope nobody can justify. Least agency is the only control in this document whose value does not depend on anyone being awake.

checks.title: Behavioural baselines for agents, with automatic containment
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S6, S8. Targets: a per-agent behavioural baseline; anomaly detection over tool-call and API patterns; kill switches that fire on the anomaly rather than waiting for a person (S10, A-34); an audit log of EVERY decision and EVERY tool call with its arguments. The failure mode guarded against: the agent whose individual actions are each perfectly legitimate but whose AGGREGATE behaviour is harmful — no single-action control will ever catch it, and no reviewer of a single diff would either. On failure: log every tool call first; you cannot baseline what you do not record. Then wire containment to the baseline, because detection alone changes nothing here.

checks.title: Quality drift detection
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S8. Targets: continuous online quality evaluation on a sample of live traffic; drift alerts kept distinct from data-drift alerts; a canary evaluation triggered automatically whenever the provider changes a model (see B-13 — this is what pinning buys you); an automatic rollback or degradation on a sustained drop. Output quality can decay with no change on your side at all. On failure: sample production traffic into the evaluation harness and alert on the trend — then wire the trend to an action, not to an inbox.

checks.title: Environment separation and parity
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1, S6. Targets: development/production parity; ephemeral per-change environments; configuration externalised from the image; an automated check, running on every deploy, that asserts no non-production environment holds a production connection string, credential or endpoint — and fails the deploy if one does. On failure: separate the data planes before anything else; then ROTATE whatever was shared (B-06); then add the assertion so it cannot silently recur.

checks.title: Feature flags and kill switches that fire themselves
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S8, S10. Targets: configuration in the environment; a feature-flag system; a documented, TESTED kill switch per AI feature, wired to an automatic tripwire on error rate, guardrail trips, cost or evaluation score. A person may also pull it; that is redundancy (S10), not the control. On failure: add the flags, then wire the tripwires. This is cheap, and it is the difference between a five-minute incident and a five-hour one — or, in a system nobody is watching, between a five-minute incident and a five-day one.

checks.title: Artifact integrity
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1, S9. Targets: signatures (Sigstore/cosign or equivalent); reproducible builds; registry access controls; verify-on-deploy that refuses unsigned artifacts and fails closed. On failure: sign and verify. Signing without verification is theatre — and in this system, theatre with no audience.

checks.title: Detection that triggers action, not just a notification
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S8, S10 — the old form of this check ended at a human, which is exactly the failure it now guards against. Targets: liveness and readiness checks; synthetic monitoring of the critical journeys; anomaly detection; an automated response bound to every alert class with a known remediation — and where an alert class has no automated response and no runbook that executes, that is a finding, not a monitoring feature; a defined mean-time-to-detect target; a break-glass escalation path defined and tested out-of-band. On failure: wire the response, not just the routing. An alert that fires into an unwatched channel is not monitoring; it is logging with extra cost — and in this system, by design, every channel is unwatched.

checks.title: Reliability primitives, validated by breaking things
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S3. Targets: circuit breakers, timeouts and backed-off retries; redundancy on the critical path; at least one chaos experiment run on a schedule against an explicit steady-state hypothesis — automated, recurring, and gating, because a chaos exercise that depends on someone remembering to run it will be run once and cited for two years. On failure: add the primitives, then run the experiment. The experiment is the evidence, not the primitives.

checks.title: Capacity, including inference capacity
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S6. Targets: autoscaling policies; a documented load and capacity plan; inference quota and headroom; graceful load-shedding that sheds the least valuable traffic first, automatically. Interaction with B-08: an autoscaler with no cost ceiling will happily scale a denial-of-wallet attack. On failure: set the policies against measured numbers, not estimated ones.

checks.title: Backups you have actually restored
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S3. Targets: a tested backup/restore with a recorded duration; documented RPO and RTO; a disaster-recovery runbook with a scheduled, automated failover test whose result is a signal rather than a memory; pipeline health monitoring. On failure: do the restore drill this week and write down what broke — something will. Then schedule it, so the next answer to 'when did we last verify the backups' is a timestamp rather than a shrug.

checks.title: Drift detection
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1. Targets: automated drift detection and reconciliation; an alert AND an automatic revert on any out-of-band manual change. On failure: enable reconciliation. Silent divergence is how a reproducible system stops being one — and a system that is no longer reproducible cannot be rebuilt by the agents that are its only maintainers.

checks.title: Integrity of the retrieval corpus
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S9. Targets: embedding and index freshness monitoring with an alert on staleness; poisoning and anomaly detection on the vector store; provenance recorded on every ingested item, so a poisoned entry can be traced, quarantined and purged rather than requiring a full rebuild. Data and model poisoning and vector/embedding weaknesses are distinct, named risk categories precisely because they persist beyond the session that introduced them — the one attack class that survives every restart in a system with no memory of its own history. On failure: add provenance at ingestion FIRST — without it you cannot clean up after a poisoning event, only rebuild from scratch.

checks.title: Latency budgets for inference
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets (engineering references, not standards — tune to the use case): time-to-first-token around ≤200ms and end-to-end ≤3000ms for interactive use; conversational interfaces tolerate ~300–500ms to first token; code completion needs <100ms; inter-token latency around 50ms (~20 tokens/second) is comfortable, and roughly 10 tokens/second already exceeds human reading speed. Track all three percentiles — the mean will lie to you. On failure: set the budget from the interaction, then optimise toward it (streaming, caching, a smaller model on the fast path).

checks.title: Release governance: segregate the gate from the gated
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S1, S9 — the one check where segregation of duties survives intact, but not in the form you expect. Targets: the policy bundle in a separate repository with separate write credentials — this is the whole check; no agent identity holding both code-write and policy-write; change approval by policy rather than by person; an immutable, append-only audit trail; record-keeping aligned to the AI-management and regulatory logging obligations that apply. On failure: separate the gate from the gated, with credentials rather than with a policy document. In a system with no human reviewer, an agent that can edit its own gate does not have a gate — it has a suggestion. Escalation: a breach of the standing control is a STOP-SHIP (§3).

checks.title: Model deprecation is a plan, not an outage
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: a gateway abstraction that makes the swap cheap; an automated deprecation-tracking process that alerts rather than a person who subscribes to a newsletter; a migration runbook; a pinned-version inventory. Actual notice periods: typically ~six months for generally-available models, three for specialised variants, and as little as two weeks for preview models — if a preview model is in your production path, that is a finding on its own. On failure: get off preview models in production; build the abstraction; automate the end-of-life tracking against an owning role.

checks.title: Retirement of AI artifacts and the right to erasure
checks.conditional_escalations: Tags: S3, S9. Targets: a retirement runbook that executes; a working right-to-erasure process reaching training data, embeddings and memory stores; a documented, enforced retention policy; an automated end-to-end erasure test running on a schedule against a canary record — a deletion path only ever exercised by hand will rot silently and you will discover it in a regulator's letter. On failure: map every derived store before you promise anyone deletion. Most systems of this kind cannot honour an erasure request and do not know it.

checks.title: Inference economics
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: prompt caching enabled with a tracked hit rate — cache reads on current frontier models from the major providers commonly cost around one tenth of the input price, making caching the single largest input-cost lever available; cost-based model routing. Structure prompts so the stable prefix is cacheable — in an agentic system the same system prompt and tool schema are re-sent thousands of times a day, and nobody is watching the invoice. On failure: enable caching, restructure the prefix, then route by cost.

checks.title: Design against a named reference framework
checks.conditional_escalations: Targets: twelve-factor adherence where relevant; a well-architected review against the six pillars; the generative-AI and responsible-AI lenses applied across the AI lifecycle phases; the gaps recorded rather than quietly dropped. On failure: run the review. Its value is the list of things nobody thought about — and that list is what this entire exercise is for.

checks.title: Core application security (authz, injection, SSRF, secrets)
Privacy & data governance
checks.conditional_escalations: priority 10 as written if EU personal data processed; else re-band vs applicable law

checks.title: LLM application risk taxonomy mapped & tested
EU AI Act inventory & classification
checks.conditional_escalations: hard gate at 10 if any AI system is high-risk on the EU market

checks.title: Sector compliance baseline
checks.conditional_escalations: applies only to frameworks that genuinely bind this product

checks.title: Agentic risk taxonomy
checks.conditional_escalations: priority 10 if any model can call a tool that writes/sends/spends/deletes/executes

checks.title: The lethal trifecta — never combine all three
Personal data in prompts, logs & traces
Guardrails have their own adversarial tests
Residency & regulatory scope at design time
AI usage policy enforced not merely published
Accountability without a signature
checks.conditional_escalations: BLOCKER-1 if any production component lacks an attested provenance chain

checks.title: Benchmark contamination & who evaluates the evaluator
